one_line_takeaway: Across 14,012 evaluations on 8 LLMs, adversarial code comments (fake audits, authority spoofing, technical deception) failed to significantly degrade vulnerability detection in any model (p > 0.21); the real gap is the 20-40pp commercial-vs-open-source baseline detection gap and structural blind spots like race conditions and timing side channels, not comment-based manipulation, and SAST-finding injection (96.9% detection, 47% recovery) beats comment stripping as a defense.
key_finding: Adversarial comments (C4) produced no statistically significant detection degradation in any of the 8 models (all McNemar exact p > 0.21), despite a 20-40 percentage-point baseline gap between commercial models (89-96% C0 detection: Sonar Pro 96.1%, Claude Opus 4.6 95.0%, GPT-5.2 93.0%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 91.9%, DeepSeek Chat 89.0%) and open-source models (53-72%: Llama 3.3 70B 72.0%, Qwen 2.5 72B 70.0%, DeepSeek-Coder-V2 16B 53.0%). Sophisticated attack variants (authority spoofing C5, attention dilution C6, technical deception C7) showed no measurable additional degradation over simple adversarial comments in properly paired analysis; an apparent 2-11 percentage-point 'backfire' improvement in unpaired comparison was traced to subset selection bias (the C5-C7 30-sample subset contained zero Java samples and had a systematically higher baseline: 98.0% vs 90.5% for the remaining 70 samples). Among defenses, SAST cross-referencing (D5) was best: 96.9% overall detection rate and 47% recovery of the 57 baseline (C4+SP0) misses, at standard single-pass API cost, with the largest per-model gain for DeepSeek (+7.6pp). Comment stripping (D1) hurt DeepSeek specifically (93.0% to 89.0%, i.e. it erased C4's apparent context benefit and reverted to the C0 uncommented baseline). D6 (comment anomaly detection) achieved 0% recovery of baseline misses. Separately, the paper identifies specific vulnerability classes that resist detection regardless of comments: TOCTOU race conditions (SAMPLE-009, CWE-367, missed by 3/5 commercial models), timing-based auth bypass via side channel (SAMPLE-011, CWE-208, missed by 4/5), and compositional Java authorization-annotation interactions (SAMPLE-020, CWE-863, missed by 3/5), plus Java cryptography detection at only 50% (n=4) and access control at 75% (n=4).
core_contribution: A large-scale empirical study (14,012 total LLM evaluations: 9,366 primary + 4,646 defense) testing whether adversarial code comments (e.g. fake 'audited by AppSec, no injection risk' claims, authority spoofing, attention dilution, technical deception citing fake CVEs/RFCs) can suppress vulnerability detection by LLM-based code reviewers. Across 8 models (5 commercial: Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.2, Gemini 2.5 Pro, DeepSeek Chat, Perplexity Sonar Pro; 3 open-source: Llama 3.3 70B, Qwen 2.5 72B, DeepSeek-Coder-V2 16B), the paper finds adversarial comments produce small, statistically non-significant detection-rate shifts (McNemar's exact p > 0.21 for all 8 models, all 95% CIs spanning zero), in sharp contrast to the 75-100% attack success rates reported for comment-based attacks against code *generation* in prior work (HACKODE, Keuper). The paper also evaluates four automated defenses and identifies a 'subset selection bias' confound that explains an apparent backfire pattern (security-themed comments appearing to correlate with higher detection).
threat_model: Attacker: a malicious insider or compromised/vendored dependency with write access to source-code comments only (not the AI review pipeline, system prompt, or scoring pipeline); gray-box (knows an LLM reviewer is used, not its exact model/prompt/defense config). Goal: increase the LLM reviewer's false-negative rate (measured as delta-FNR) on specific vulnerabilities by inserting deceptive comments (fake audit sign-offs, fictional JIRA tickets, claims that a WAF/API gateway already handles security) while leaving executable code functionally identical. Explicitly out of scope: adaptive attackers who iterate against a known target model, combined comment+code-obfuscation attacks, and multi-file attacks. Defender: a single LLM deployed in CI/CD with a standard security-review system prompt (SP0) and no SAST integration, multi-pass analysis, or comment-aware preprocessing as the baseline deployment.
limitations: All 100 benchmark samples are synthetic, single-vulnerability, and short (typically 15-60 lines); the paper explicitly disclaims external validity to large-scale production codebases and claims only paired robustness on controlled single-vulnerability samples. Keyword-based automated scoring can undercount detections using non-standard phrasing or overcount C5-C7 responses via comment echoing (bounded by the 7.1% audited echo rate, though the paper argues paired delta-FNR comparisons cancel this bias). False positive rates were not measured at all -- only false negatives. The adversary modeled is static/non-adaptive; a real attacker iterating against a known target model, combining comment attacks with code obfuscation, or attacking across multiple files is explicitly out of scope. Sonar Pro's web search was left enabled (provider default), making it not strictly closed-book comparable to the other commercial models, though the authors report manual inspection found no evidence this affected outcomes. The commercial run had 417 API errors (out of 8,766, from Perplexity API instability). Models tested reflect a February 2026 snapshot and both capabilities and vulnerabilities evolve rapidly. The C5-C7 sophisticated-attack subset (first 30 samples by index, zero Java) is a real confound that the paper itself surfaces and partially, not fully, disentangles.

datasets_used: Custom 100-sample synthetic vulnerability benchmark authored by the first author (50 Python, 30 JavaScript, 20 Java; 91 unique vulnerability types; 28 critical/55 high/17 medium severity), verified via Bandit, ESLint security plugins, SpotBugs/FindSecBugs
quantitative_results: 9,366 primary evaluations + 4,646 defense evaluations = 14,012 total evaluations (~15,200 API calls)
All 8 models: adversarial comments (C4) vs baseline (C0), McNemar exact p > 0.21 for every model (non-significant)
Commercial C0 baseline detection: Sonar Pro 96.1%, Claude Opus 4.6 95.0%, GPT-5.2 93.0%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 91.9%, DeepSeek Chat 89.0%
Open-source C0 baseline detection: Llama 3.3 70B 72.0%, Qwen 2.5 72B 70.0%, DeepSeek-Coder-V2 16B 53.0%
SAST cross-referencing (D5) defense: 96.9% overall detection rate, 47% recovery (27/57) of baseline misses
Comment stripping (D1): 93.2% overall DR, 30% recovery (17/57); hurt DeepSeek specifically (93.0% to 89.0%)
Comment anomaly detection (D6): 95.1% overall DR, 0% recovery (0/57)
Keyword-scoring audit (n=74 responses): 85.1% keyword-match rate; C5-C7 comment-echo (false hit) rate 7.1% (1/14, 95% CI 0-20.7%); paraphrase-miss rate 9.1% (1/11)
Subset selection bias: C0 detection on the 30-sample C5-C7 subset vs full 100-sample baseline, e.g. Claude Opus 4.6 95.0% to 100.0% (+5.0pp), Gemini 2.5 Pro 91.9% to 100.0% (+8.1pp)
SP1 (skeptical prompt) recovered 43% of SP0's C4-induced misses; SP2 (method-enforced) increased misses relative to SP1
28 total induced misses across C4 conditions: 14 under C4+SP0 (11 unique samples), 4 under C4+SP1, 10 under C4+SP2
Java cryptography detection 50% (n=4) and access control 75% (n=4) vs Java injection detection 100% (n=7), showing category-specific rather than general language weakness
Open-source evaluation: 600/600 evaluations completed with zero errors; commercial run had 417 errors out of 8,766 (mostly Perplexity API instability)
baselines_compared: 8 comment variants: C0 no comments, C1 accurate comments, C2 neutral comments, C3 misleading comments, C4 adversarial comments, C5 authority spoofing, C6 attention dilution, C7 technical deception (C5-C7 on 30-sample subset only)
3 system prompt conditions: SP0 naive, SP1 skeptical ('treat comments as UNTRUSTED'), SP2 method-enforced structured methodology
4 defenses: D1 comment stripping, D2 dual-pass analysis, D5 SAST cross-referencing, D6 comment anomaly detection (regex-based)
Commercial vs open-source model classes (5 vs 3 models) as a baseline-capability comparison, not a defense baseline
